deepseek-r1-distill-llama-8b.text: Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this 61-year-old man with eyelid swelling.
Let me go through the information step by step.

First, the patient has a history of glaucoma, which is important because glaucoma can cause optic nerve damage, leading to things like increased cup-to-disk ratio, which was noted in the funduscopic exam.
He's been having progressively worsening eyelid swelling for five years, which started after his glaucoma was diagnosed.
The ophthalmologist suspected dermatochalasis with fat prolapse, and he was referred for blepharoplasty.
But the swelling is interfering with his daily activities like reading and driving, so it's more than just a cosmetic issue.
He also has HIV, which is well-controlled with antiretroviral therapy, but he has lipodystrophy.
Lipodystrophy can cause fat redistribution, including in the orbits, which might contribute to orbital issues.
He's had other conditions like carpal tunnel syndrome, cataracts, depression, dyslipidemia, hypertension, allergic rhinitis, obstructive sleep apnea, and asthma.
He's had several surgeries: carpal tunnel release, cataract removal, and a lipectomy for lipodystrophy-related issues.
His medications include antiretroviral therapy, bupropion, fluoxetine, triamterene, amlodipine, atorvastatin, inhaled albuterol, and topical testosterone.
He's a nonsmoker, drinks occasionally, and doesn't use illicit substances.
He lives part-time in Florida and New England.
On exam, his corrected visual acuity is 20/25 and 20/30, which is a bit different but not severely impacting vision yet.
Pupils are reactive, so no issue there.
Confrontation testing shows visual field deficits in the outer superior temporal and superior nasal fields, which improve with lid elevation.
That makes me think of ptosis or something affecting the superior visual fields, maybe related to the eyelid edema.
He has soft edema and erythema of the upper and lower eyelids, with overhanging skin, indicating dermatochalasis.
Ptosis is present, and the levator muscles are impaired, with measurements below the normal range.
Slit-lamp shows chemosis and injection, but no keratopathy or anterior chamber cells.
Fundus exam shows the glaucoma changes, so that's consistent.
His MRI and CT scans show orbital fat stranding, extraocular muscle enlargement, and preseptal thickening.
The short-tau inversion recovery sequence shows increased signal in the muscles, sparing the myotendinous junctions.
This suggests myositis or inflammation in the muscles.
The perineuritis around the optic nerves adds to the picture.
Blood work shows slightly elevated ESR and IgG levels, but other autoimmune markers like ANCA and IgG4 are normal.
No evidence of thyroid issues either.
The lack of response to contrast in the CT and the MRI findings point towards an orbital myositis rather than infection or other processes.
Putting it all together, the main issues are the eyelid swelling, ptosis, impaired levator function, orbital inflammation, and MRI findings of myositis.
This is classic for orbital myositis, which is an inflammatory condition affecting the muscles around the eyes.
It's often idiopathic but can be associated with systemic conditions like HIV or autoimmune diseases, but in this case, the workup doesn't show those.
So the most likely diagnosis is orbital myositis.

deepseek-r1-distill-qwen-14b.text: Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this 61-year-old man with eyelid swelling and other symptoms.
Let me go through the case step by step.
First, the patient has a history of mild puffiness in the eyelids that started eight years ago, getting worse over time.
He was initially diagnosed with dermatochalasis with fat prolapse and referred for blepharoplasty.
But now, the swelling is more severe, affecting his vision, and he's having other symptoms like pressure behind the eyes, double vision, and joint stiffness.
Looking at his medical history, he has HIV for 31 years, well-controlled with antiretroviral therapy, but he has lipodystrophy.
He's had surgeries like carpal tunnel release and lipectomy for dorsocervical lipohypertrophy.
He's on several medications, including antiretrovirals, antidepressants, and others.
On exam, he has ptosis, edema, and erythema of the eyelids, with limited eye movement.
Imaging shows enlarged extraocular muscles and fat stranding in the orbits.
MRI shows orbital fat stranding and muscle enlargement, with high signal intensity sparing the myotendinous junctions.
There's also perineuritis around the optic nerves.
His lab results show elevated IgG2 levels, normal IgG1 and IgG3, and normal ACE, C-reactive protein, and ESR except for a slightly elevated ESR.
ANCA and other tests are negative.
Putting this together, the symptoms of eyelid swelling, eye muscle enlargement, and orbital fat changes suggest an orbital inflammatory condition.
The elevated IgG2 and the imaging findings point towards a diagnosis of IgG4-related disease.
This condition often presents with orbital inflammation, elevated IgG4 levels, and can affect multiple systems.
The patient's symptoms, especially the orbital involvement and elevated IgG2, align with this diagnosis.
